Results.CopyCode:
"""

This is a command line interface tool to make using
the tiktok-downloader from the link below easier and
more efficient.

https://github.com/n0l3r/tiktok-downloader

tiktok-downloader and tiktoks should be on your main
path for this to work. Ex: /Users/username/tiktoks

"""

import os
import subprocess
from termcolor import colored as c
import requests
import re
#This command worked to install bs4 -> sudo python3 -m pip install bs4

def get_website_link(app_link: str) -> str:

	# Get a response from the website
	response = requests.get(app_link)

	if response.status_code == 200:
		# Use regex to find link in giant HTML response.
		website_link_info = re.search(r'"canonical":"https:.+","pageId"', response.text)

		if website_link_info:
			website_link = str(website_link_info.group(0))
			return website_link.replace('"canonical":"', '').replace('","pageId', '').replace(r'/', '/').strip()
		else:
			print(c("Website link not found in the HTML.", 'red'))
			return None
	else:
		print(c(f"Failed to fetch the website link. Status code: {response.status_code}", 'red'))
		return None

def write_to_clipboard(output: str):
	process = subprocess.Popen('pbcopy', env={'LANG': 'en_US.UTF-8'}, stdin=subprocess.PIPE)
	process.communicate(output.encode('utf-8'))

print(c("Remember that you can NOT download slideshows", 'red'))
print(c("The website link will be fetched using the app link and copied to the clipboard.", 'blue'))
print(c("Later when prompted to \"Paste the URL\" paste the website link.", 'cyan'))

link = input("Enter TikTok app link: ")

if link.strip():
	website_link = get_website_linklink #remove last " char

	write_to_clipboard(website_link)
	print(c("Website link copied to clipboard", 'green'), end="")
	print(f": {website_link}")
	#webbrowser.open(link.strip())

os.chdir("../tiktok-downloader")

def time_to_seconds(time) -> int:
	""" Converts int seconds to formatted time. """
	t = [int(x) for x in time.split(":")]
	return t[1] + (t[0] * 60) if (len(t) == 2) else t[2] + (t[1] * 60) + (t[0] * 3600)

def seconds_to_time(seconds) -> str:
	""" Converts seconds to minutes """
	if seconds < 60:
		return f"0:{seconds:02}"
	elif seconds < 3600:
		minutes = seconds // 60
		seconds %= 60
		return f"{minutes}:{seconds:02}"
	else:
		hours = seconds // 3600
		seconds %= 3600
		minutes = seconds // 60
		seconds %= 60
		return f"{hours}:{minutes:02}:{seconds:02}"

from flask import Blueprint, jsonify, request
import requests
from config import CLIENT_ID, CLIENT_SECRET
from get_access_token import get_access_token

routes_blueprint = Blueprint('routes', __name__)

@routes_blueprint.route('/search', methods=['POST'])
def search():
	album_title = request.form.get('album_title')
	access_token = get_access_token(CLIENT_ID, CLIENT_SECRET)
	headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"}
	search_url = "https://api.spotify.com/v1/search"
	search_params = {"q": album_title, "type": "album", "limit": 1}

	try:
		search_resp = requests.get(search_url, headers=headers, params=search_params)
		search_resp.raise_for_status()
		search_results = search_resp.json()
		albums = search_results.get('albums', {}).get('items', [])

		if not albums:
			return jsonify({"error": "No albums found with that title."}), 404

		selected_album = albums[0]
		album_id = selected_album['id']
		album_name = selected_album['name']

		tracks_url = f"https://api.spotify.com/v1/albums/{album_id}/tracks"
		tracks_resp = requests.get(tracks_url, headers=headers)
		tracks_resp.raise_for_status()
		tracks_data = tracks_resp.json()
		tracks = [track['name'] for track in tracks_data['items']]

		return jsonify({"album_name": album_name, "tracks": tracks})
	except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
		return jsonify({"error": "Failed to fetch data from Spotify API."}), 500

Results.CopyCode:
import requests
import json
from dotenv import load_dotenv
import os

load_dotenv()

CLIENT_ID = os.getenv('CLIENT_ID')
CLIENT_SECRET = os.getenv('CLIENT_SECRET')
REDIRECT_URI = os.getenv('REDIRECT_URI')
AUTH_CODE = os.getenv('AUTH_CODE')


def get_access_token(authorization_code):
	token_url = "https://www.linkedin.com/oauth/v2/accessToken"
	payload = {
		'grant_type': 'authorization_code',
		'code': authorization_code,
		'redirect_uri': REDIRECT_URI,
		'client_id': CLIENT_ID,
		'client_secret': CLIENT_SECRET
	}
	response = requests.post(token_url, data=payload)
	access_token = response.json().get('access_token')
	return access_token


def get_profile_data(access_token):
	profile_url = "https://api.linkedin.com/v2/userinfo"
	headers = {
		"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"
	}
	response = requests.get(profile_url, headers=headers)
	profile_data = response.json()
	return profile_data


def main():
	access_token = get_access_token(AUTH_CODE)
	profile_data = get_profile_data(access_token)
	print("Your account information")
	print(json.dumps(profile_data, indent=2))

if __name__ == "__main__":
	main()

Results.CopyCode:
import os
import requests
import webbrowser
from urllib.parse import urlencode
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

CLIENT_ID = os.getenv('CLIENT_ID')
CLIENT_SECRET = os.getenv('CLIENT_SECRET')
REDIRECT_URI = 'http://localhost:8080/callback'
DURATION = 'permanent'
SCOPE = 'identity read'

creds = {
	'client_id': CLIENT_ID,
	'response_type': 'code',
	'state': 'random_string',
	'redirect_uri': REDIRECT_URI,
	'duration': DURATION,
	'scope': SCOPE
}

auth_url = f"https://www.reddit.com/api/v1/authorize?{urlencode(creds)}"

print(f"Go to the following URL to authorize your bot: {auth_url}")
webbrowser.open(auth_url)

auth_code = input("Enter the authorization code: ")

auth = requests.auth.HTTPBasicAuth(CLIENT_ID, CLIENT_SECRET)

data = {
	'grant_type': 'authorization_code',
	'code': auth_code,
	'redirect_uri': REDIRECT_URI
}

headers = {'User-Agent': 'MyBot/0.0.1'}

res = requests.post('https://www.reddit.com/api/v1/access_token',
					 auth=auth, data=data, headers=headers)

response_json = res.json()
refresh_token = response_json['refresh_token']
print(f"\033[94mYour refresh token is\033[0m: {refresh_token}")

Results.instruction: Now open two terminals. In the first terminal run the two following commands to start the server.
In the second server run the following commands to execute your script. A webpage will be opened.
Press the orange "Allow" button on the webpage. Then copy and record the authorization code.

Go back to your second terminal and paste in the authorization code where it's asking for input. The refresh token will then be printed. COPY AND RECORD IT. Do not use the authorization token in the next step.

Close all your open terminals. Then, open your ".env" file and replace "your_refresh_token" with the value you just copied. This will allow OAuth2 to work without having to use a new 2FA code every time your run your script.
Paste the following code in the "reddit_bot.py" file. Make sure to change the value of the "subreddit_name" variable.

Results.CopyCode:
import praw
import smtplib
from email.mime.multipart import MIMEMultipart
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

# Your Reddit credentials
CLIENT_ID = os.getenv('CLIENT_ID')
CLIENT_SECRET = os.getenv('CLIENT_SECRET')
REFRESH_TOKEN = os.getenv('REFRESH_TOKEN')
USER_AGENT = os.getenv('USER_AGENT')

# Your Email Credentials
sender_email = os.getenv('SENDER_EMAIL')
sender_password = os.getenv('SENDER_PASSWORD')
email_recipient = os.getenv('EMAIL_RECIPIENT')

# Name of Subreddit you're working with. AskReddit is good for testing
subreddit_name = 'NAME_OF_SUBREDDIT'


reddit = praw.Reddit(
	client_id=CLIENT_ID,
	client_secret=CLIENT_SECRET,
	refresh_token=REFRESH_TOKEN,
	user_agent=USER_AGENT
)


subreddit = reddit.subreddit(subreddit_name)

def send_email(title):

	subject = f"Someone posted in r/{subreddit_name}!"

	body = (
		f'The title of the post is "{title}".\n\n\n' + 
		"This was sent from you're deployed reddit bot."
	)

	message = MIMEMultipart()
	message["From"] = sender_email
	message["To"] = email_recipient
	message["Subject"] = subject

	message.attach(MIMEText(body, "plain"))

	try:
		server = smtplib.SMTP("smtp.gmail.com", 587)
		server.starttls()
		server.login(sender_email, sender_password)
		text = message.as_string()
		server.sendmail(sender_email, email_recipient, text)
		server.quit()
		print("Email sent successfully!")
	except Exception as e:
		print(f"Failed to send email. Error: {e}")

def main():
	print(f"Monitoring new posts in r/{subreddit_name}...")

	for submission in subreddit.stream.submissions(skip_existing=True):
		print(f"\nSomeone made a post titled: \033[94m{submission.title}\033[0m")
		send_email(submission.title)


if __name__ == "__main__":

	if subreddit_name == 'NAME_OF_SUBREDDIT':
		print('\033[91mChange value of subreddit_name variable\033[0m')

	main()

from sqlalchemy import Column, Integer, String, Sequence
from database import Base

class Item(Base):
	__tablename__ = 'items'

	name = Column(String(50))

Results.instruction: In the "handler.py" file paste the following code.

Results.CopyCode:
import http.server
import json
from database import session, init_db
from models import Item

class CustomHandler(http.server.SimpleHTTPRequestHandler):
	def do_GET(self):
		if self.path == '/items':
			self.send_response(200)
			self.send_header("Content-type", "application/json")
			self.end_headers()

			items = session.query(Item).all()
			items_list = [{"id": item.id, "name": item.name} for item in items]
			self.wfile.write(json.dumps(items_list).encode('utf-8'))
		else:
			super().do_GET()

	def do_POST(self):
		if self.path == '/add-item':
			content_length = int(self.headers['Content-Length'])
			post_data = self.rfile.read(content_length)
			data = json.loads(post_data.decode('utf-8'))

			new_item = Item(name=data['name'])
			session.add(new_item)
			session.commit()
			self.send_response(200)
			self.send_header("Content-type", "application/json")
			self.end_headers()
			response = {"message": "Item added successfully", "id": new_item.id}
			self.wfile.write(json.dumps(response).encode('utf-8'))
		else:
			self.send_response(404)
			self.end_headers()

init_db()

import sqlite3

conn = sqlite3.connect('Cars.db')

cursor = conn.cursor()

cursor.execute('''
	CREATE TABLE Cars (
		Maker VARCHAR(20),
		Color VARCHAR(20),
		Year INT
	);
''')

conn.commit()
conn.close()

print("\033[92m" + "Cars table successfully created!" + "\033[0m")

Results.instruction: Open the "populate_db.py" file and paste in the following code.

Results.CopyCode:
import sqlite3
import json

with open("cars.json", 'r') as file:
	contents = file.read()
	data = json.loads(contents)

conn = sqlite3.connect('Cars.db')

cursor = conn.cursor()

for car in data['Cars']:

	maker = car["Maker"]
	color = car["Color"]
	year = car["Year"]

	sql = 'INSERT INTO Cars (maker, color, year) VALUES (?, ?, ?)'

	cursor.execute(sql, (maker, color, year))

conn.commit()
conn.close()

print("\033[92m" + "Cars table successfully populated!" + "\033[0m")

# Name: Michael Totaro
# Date: 10/24/2022
# Purpose: Create and demonstrate CarList class (Not a real assignment).
# collaborators/resources: None other than Dr. Pence

class Car:
	""" This class is a basic car object. """
	def __init__(self, car_id, company, year, color, miles):
		"""
		Initializer for a Car object.
		:param id: Id of the car
		:param company: Maker of the car
		:param year: Year car was made
		:param color: Color of the car
		:param miles: Miles driven by the car
		"""
		self.id = car_id
		self.company = company
		self.year = year
		self.color = color
		self.miles = miles

	def __str__(self):
		"""
		Creates a string representation of a car object.
		:return: string representation of a car object.
		"""
		car_str = (f"\nCar {self.id}\n"
				   f"Company: {self.company}\n"
				   f"Year: {self.year}\n"
				   f"Color: {self.color}\n"
				   f"Miles: {self.miles}")

		return car_str + "\n"


class CarList:
	"""
	This class is data structure of car objects.
	You can add, remove, and print the list.
	"""
	def __init__(self):
		"""
		Initializes an empty car list
		"""
		self.car_list = []

	def add_car(self, new_car):
		"""
		Adds a car to the car list if conditions are met.
		:param new_car: Car object to be added to the list
		:return: True if the car was added successfully else false.
		:raises ValueError: If parameter is not an instance of a car object
		"""

		if not isinstance(new_car, Car):
			raise ValueError("Parameter must be an instance of a car object.")

		for car in self.car_list:
			if car.id == new_car.id:
				print(f"A car in the list already has the id '{new_car.id}'!")
				return False

		self.car_list.append(new_car)

		return True

	def remove_car_by_id(self, car_id):
		"""
		Removes car in the list by id.
		:param car_id: The id of the car to be removed.
		:raises ValueError: If parameter is not an int.
		"""

		if not isinstance(car_id, int):
			raise ValueError("car_id must be an integer.")

		for car in self.car_list:
			if car.id == car_id:
				self.car_list.remove(car)
				print(f"Car {car_id} removed successfully!")
				return

		print(f"No car with the id {car_id} was found!")

	def __str__(self):
		"""
		Creates a string representation of a CarList object.
		:return: The cars in the list as a string
		"""
		return "\n" + " ".join([str(car) for car in self.car_list])

	def __len__(self):
		"""
		Get the length of the car_list
		:return: The length of the car list
		"""
		return len(self.car_list)


def main():
	"""
	The driver code to test the CarList class.
	"""
	car_list = CarList()

	car_list.add_car(Car(1, "Honda", 2012, "gray", 12430))
	car_list.add_car(Car(2, "Toyota", 2005, "black", 25430))
	car_list.add_car(Car(3, "Ford", 1999, "red", 19430))

	try:
		car_list.add_car("This is a string object")
	except ValueError as e:
		print(e)

	car_list.add_car(Car(1, "Used id", 1999, "red", 19430))

	print(f"Length of list before you remove a car {len(car_list)}")

	car_list.remove_car_by_id(1)

	print(f"Length of list after you remove a car {len(car_list)}")

	car_list.remove_car_by_id(100000)

	print(car_list)

main()

Results.desc: These are more general tips I have for school and life in general.

Results.tip: No matter what I'm working on, it always seems like the hardest part of it is getting started. I find that I sometimes procrastinate when I find a task daunting or think it will be challenging. It's never as bad it seems once you get started and get into the flow of things. Just try your best to start working on what you find difficult and it's usually not nearly as hard as you think it will be.

This is kind of adding the first point, learn the 5 second rule. Whenever you have something to do and are procrastinating, don't just sit there and ponder about it. Start counting down from 5 and when you get to 1, make a decision about what you're going to do. For example, say I've been scrolling on Instagram for a while and have work to do. I'd start counting down from 5 and once I get to 1 I'll decide if I should keep scrolling or go do something more productive. It works basically everytime.

The beauty of UNCW's campus is extremely underrated imo. App State and UNC tend to get all the attention. However, UNCW has some really nice, very big hiking trails I used to walk somewhat frequently. It's a great way to get exercise and unwind at the same time. I also think it'd be a fun activity to do with your friends. The trails I'm referring to are behind Seahawk Landing, though I'd typically start at a different point.

Results.underlined: Send in your high school AP scores
Results.tip: If you haven't done so already, I'd recommend sending in your high school AP scores. They can get you college credit which can be beneficial for many reasons. The credit can help you graduate early, get out of classes you don't want to take, give you more time to take classes you do want to take, etc.
Internships are a great way to develop skills and gain experience. It also looks good to future employers who are looking for someone with past experience.
It's normal to think that everyone knows more than you, or is more experienced than you. Don't be afraid to ask for help or feel lesser of yourself. People will typically be happy to help you learn.

Results.underlined: Keep up to date with you emails
Results.tip: Sometimes emails contain important information that requires your attention. I'd recommend checking and cleaning your emails consistently to keep up with important info.

Whenever I had to give a presentation in college it always seemed like people were missing a cable to hookup their computer to the smartboard. I think cable adapters are a great thing to have to charge multiple devices at once, hookup to smartboard, etc. I'd recommend buying this one that you can buy from your older brothers company Amazon. It's a little pricey but definitely worth it imo. Just make sure to buy one that actually works for your computer.
Your GPA doesn't have to be perfect, but companies usually ask what your GPA is using ranges. The top range is usually 3.5 and above, so try to maintain that.

Obviously if you think this would be too heavy a workload, you don't have to take this many credit hours. However if you want to graduate early taking 21 credits in a semester might help you. I did this a couple times in college and it allowed to me graduate a semester early. Contact your counselor for an override.

Results.underlined: Buy your diploma frame from the UNCW bookstore
Results.tip: The bookstore sells diploma frames. When you graduate it's a good place to look since they have the right sizes.

Results.underlined: Have a .txt file on your computer paste information
Results.tip: Having a single text file you can temporarily put information in has been useful in my experience. You can shrink it and quickly copy, paste, and delete info while you're working. You can have it open right beside whatever you're working on.

Results.underlined: Network and meet people at your job
Results.tip: Networking is a great way to meet new people and gain new opportunities.
This is a relatively affordable way to get from Wilmington to Charlotte if you don't have a car.

Results.underlined: Follow Lil Goof Ball on TikTok
Results.tip: This guy went to UNCW and I think his TikTok is pretty funny. You can follow him here. Also, he's on campus pretty frequently, so keep an eye out for him if you want.
Finishing one task at a time can help keep you focused and organized on what you're working on.

Having a well-organized and professional looking resume can help you stand out to employers. I've gotten a couple compliments on my resume from recruiters before. See this page for the template I use.

Results.underlined: It's not too late to change major, or add a minor
Results.tip: If you wanted to switch to computer science, or add a CS as a minor it's not too late. I outlined classes you be taking in this [page](/Classes/CompSciMinor). If you switched you could also use some of the classes you've probably already taken for a business concentration/minor.

Results.underlined: Know how to present a powerpoint project with notes on zoom
Results.tip: To share your presentation on zoom without showing your audience the presenters notes watch this video. First open your powerpoint presentation, then on the right-side of the bottom bar, click on the icon that says "Slide Show" when hovered over and click on it. Then in the lower left-hand side click on the icon with three dots, in the pop up menu select "Use Presenter View". Now go back to zoom using control + up arrow (if you’re on mac). When you're back on zoom click "Share" and select the screen that says "Microsoft PowerPoint - PowerPoint Slide Show" or something similar when hovered over. Then click "Share", you should be good now.

Results.description: A project called "plane-notify" created by Jack Sweeney. This video does a really good job describing what it is. It's basically a bot that tracks Elon Musks jet. It's written python and you can see the source code here. Also, Jack Sweeney accepted my connect request on Linkedin which is pretty cool.

Search bars. Ever since I started coding I've had a somewhat strange fascination with search bars. It seems like they're everywhere but people still have difficulty implementing them in their projects. That's why I made sure not to "cheat" the search bar functionality in this project (it was originally supposed to be a box pop-up thing). Search bars can be extremely useful in your project and I'd recommend implementing them wherever you think they'd be helpful.

Steve Jobs talking about programming. In this video Steve Jobs talks about how he thinks everyone should learn to code. I like the point he makes about how programming teaches you how to think in the real world. I often find myself using Java syntax to describe real-life events in my head.

GitHub pages. I love GitHub pages and it's where this website is currently being hosted. It allows you to host any static website for free which I find very convenient as my websites usually don't use databases or dynamic rendering.
Pushing to GitHub. I like the feeling of pushing code to GitHub and have it successfully working. I find it satisfying.
Visual Studio Code is the main text editor I use. I find it very versatile and I use it in basically all my programming tasks. I'd strongly recommend downloading it and trying it out.

The UNCW Computer Science curriculum. I think UNCW has a great CS curriculum that allows for a lot of creativity and flexibility. It also teaches you a diverse spectrum of skills which will help prepare you for the real world.
Markdown. I really like markdowns syntax and I find it convenient when creating ReadMes. I took inspiration from markdowns syntax for the hyperlinks in this project.
Emailjs. This is a JavaScript library that allows you send emails for free. I used it in the "Contact Me" page in this project. Here's the link to set it up.
The "termcolor" library. This is a Python library that allows you to color the text in your terminal output. I find it very useful to differentiate between messages and I think it looks nice.
